risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The company randomized the investigational product. The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected." Comment: The paper states that randomization was performed and that the allocation information was concealed from researchers and participants until study completion. Although the exact algorithm is not described, this is sufficient context to indicate a proper random allocation process, so low risk is appropriate.

Quote: "The company randomized the investigational product. The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected." Comment: This explicitly states that the randomization information was withheld from researchers and participants until study completion, which indicates an allocation process that prevented foreknowledge of upcoming assignments.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected." Comment: This indicates that both participants and personnel were blinded to allocation, and the key outcomes were subjective appearance/self-report outcomes that could be influenced by awareness of treatment; therefore successful blinding lowers performance bias risk.

Quote: "The secondary goal of this study was to evaluate whether the topical product decreased the appearance of the facial angiofibromas after 6 months of usage, as self-reported by the subjects." and "Upon completion of the trial, subjects were asked if they ‘got better on the treatment’, if they ‘got worse on the treatment’, or if ‘the treatment made no difference’." Comment: The outcome assessor for efficacy was the patient self-report (the patients themselves assessed whether their skin appearance improved). The study reports double-blinding, but the key efficacy outcome is subjective and patient-reported; if patients were unblinded or blinding was compromised, knowledge of allocation could directly influence this measurement. Because this outcome is subjective and the paper does not explicitly describe blinding of the outcome assessment beyond a general double-blind statement, detection bias is at high risk.

Quote: "Two subjects withdrew from the study, secondary to discomfort with product application (a burning sensation with application). Two subjects were withdrawn by the investigators, secondary to poor compliance with study protocols. One subject was withdrawn following prolonged hospitalization unrelated to the study product. Twenty-three subjects completed the entire study protocol." Comment: Attrition was limited (5/28 total withdrawals, about 18%) and reasons were reported, with one withdrawal due to unrelated hospitalization and the others due to discomfort or poor compliance. The report does not suggest marked imbalance or outcome-related missingness, so the incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
